summary: Deprecated audit trail for DpcWatchdogPeriod. The current app writes HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Kernel\DpcWatchdogPeriod = 120000, and the decompiled watchdog query path now shows the raw period value being consumed, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact registry key and value semantics.

setting.name: DPC Watchdog Period
setting.casual_explanation: Windows has a watchdog that looks for deferred procedure calls that run too long and can trigger a system crash if the system stays stuck at high interrupt levels. This is a very low-level kernel safety mechanism, not a normal tweak.
setting.why_people_change_it: People would only change it when reproducing kernel experiments, undoing manual registry changes, or doing specialized driver debugging.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Changing watchdog timing can affect how quickly Windows decides a DPC path has stalled badly enough to stop the system. Because this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact registry key, the safe choice is to keep it in review.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents that Windows implements a DPC watchdog timer and that a DPC_WATCHDOG_VIOLATION (0x133) bug check occurs if a DPC routine or a series of DPC routines runs too long. Microsoft also provides driver guidance for avoiding DPC watchdog timeouts in storport miniports. The decompiled watchdog query path shows KeDpcWatchdogPeriodMs being read into the configuration block, but this research pass still did not capture a primary Microsoft page that documents the registry value DpcWatchdogPeriod itself. The repo research notes list DpcWatchdogPeriod = 120000 and associate it with KeDpcWatchdogPeriodMs, so this record keeps the feature area documented but blocks validated publication of the registry mapping.

allowed_values.label: Primary Microsoft registry mapping not captured

allowed_values.meaning: The current app treats 120000 as the watchdog-period baseline, based on repo research rather than a captured primary Microsoft registry source.

windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems where this dataset has not yet validated DpcWatchdogPeriod from a primary Microsoft source

states.rationale: Do not publish a validated watchdog-period baseline until the exact registry mapping is backed by a primary Microsoft source.

windows_defaults.notes: This keeps the low-level watchdog setting out of published presets until it is properly sourced.

recommended_profiles.label: Hold for research

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to document the watchdog setting without overstating confidence.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a primary-source Microsoft-backed watchdog-period control.
tradeoffs: The DPC watchdog feature area is documented honestly.
The low-level registry value stays blocked from validated publication.

The exact registry semantics remain based on repo provenance rather than a captured primary source.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: KeQueryDpcWatchdogInformation function
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents that Windows implements a DPC watchdog timer and describes how the system detects prolonged DPC execution and can bug check with DPC_WATCHDOG_VIOLATION (0x133). The page confirms the feature area but does not publish the DpcWatchdogPeriod registry key.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Avoiding DPC Watchdog timeout problems in StorPort Miniports
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the DPC watchdog feature from the driver-behavior side and explains that Windows can decide a prolonged high-IRQL routine has taken excessive time to finish. The page confirms the watchdog feature area but does not publish the registry key.

evidence.title: Decompiled DPC watchdog configuration reader
evidence.summary: The decompiled watchdog configuration reader checks KeDpcWatchdogPeriodMs and copies it into the result block when present, proving the raw registry-backed period is consumed by kernel code.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for kernel registry values
evidence.summary: The repo documents DpcWatchdogPeriod = 120000 as personal research and ties it to KeDpcWatchdogPeriodMs.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DpcWatchdogPeriod = 120000 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Decompiled DPC watchdog configuration reader
evidence.summary: The decompiled watchdog configuration reader checks KeDpcWatchdogPeriodMs and copies it into the result block when present, proving the raw watchdog period value is consumed by kernel code.

decision.why: Microsoft clearly documents the DPC watchdog feature, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the DpcWatchdogPeriod registry value itself.
blocking_issues: No primary Microsoft source for the DpcWatchdogPeriod registry value was captured in this research pass.
The current app mapping relies on repo provenance instead of a directly captured Microsoft source for the registry key.

open_questions: Can a primary Microsoft source for DpcWatchdogPeriod be captured?
Should the app surface this only as an experimental kernel watchdog setting?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: if ( KeDpcWatchdogPeriodMs ) { ... LODWORD(Src) = Src | 0x200; DWORD2(Src) = KeDpcWatchdogPeriodMs; }
validation_proof.notes: Kernel DPC watchdog audit trail. The decompiled routine shows the raw watchdog period value being queried and copied into the configuration block.
